author: @PHDTS
text: I've been using the TORRAS Magnetic Slim Fit case on my iPhone 14 Pro for 3 years, and now have one on my 17 Pro since the magnet is one of the strongest I've tried.  It's probably the most slim TPU case I've found that protects the camera well and is sleek & clean looking.  It's a little slippery at first but within a couple of days it becomes nice & grippy and never gets sticky feeling.  I've dropped my 14 Pro a handful of times onto hard surfaces over the years and it protected the phone each time.  The main thing is the magnet was super strong on these cases, way stronger than the Tech Woven case that I had originally bought for my 17 Pro but I've since returned it.  The TORRAS Magnetic Slim Fit case holds more firmly onto the magsafe style mounts in my cars than the Tech Woven case did (more shaking with that case).

author: @zenrand688
text: Though I know it’s difficult to measure (I’m a wireless engineer), what all of these case reviews lack is the impact to cellular, WiFi, and BT reception. Some cases can be very detrimental to wireless performance - the ones Apple sells (1st & 3rd party) are tested for wireless performance so that is an advantage imo.  Carbon fiber for example will block wireless energy so the case design is really important when this material is used. Given the wireless performance is a key aspect of phone functionality, I would hope these case reviewers start paying attention to that.

author: @MasonI24
text: Yeah I’m wondering how these metal camera rings will interfere with connectivity since the plateau is surrounded by antenna lines.

author: @AL-lh2ht
text: How would you theoretically test wireless performance?

author: @zenrand688
text: @AL-lh2htProbably easiest to test BT and WiFi - you would need a reference WiFi AP for example and place a phone at a given distance. Test several orientations with multiple iterations so you could get an average for each orientation. With WiFi you could use an app that exposes the Phy rate (eg unifi) or use a sniffer to get logs. This is somewhat abstracted but good enough to get relative impact of a case vs. no case (as a baseline). With WiFi you can test 2.4GHz and 5-6GHz so that gives you some idea of the frequency response of the case.

So, what's the best clear (no white block on the back) case for iPhone 17 Pro?

Protective.
Thin.
Good button feel/responsiveness.
Non-yellowing.
Strong Magsafe magnet.

author: @forthphoto
text:
First - I really appreciate your videos. But I bought both this spigen and slightly more expensive ESR and:
Apart from numbers which I am not able to test, it’s pretty much worse in every way from my slightly more expensive ESR. 
Glass protection - ESR is deep on top and bottom making it usable from the corners and safer when face dropped. 
Camera button - it’s decent on ESR. 
Buttons - just as bad. 
Camera protection - much better on ESR. 
ESR is just as good with MagSafe charger from apple but better when mounting it on my steel squat rack.
All of that for £2 more than this spigen.
I’ve ordered both and returned spigen. ESR also feels better on the corners but I won’t drop it on purpose. 

That said - I really appreciate your reviews and it’s possible that we talk about different ESR case - I bought mine in UK for ~£17 while spigen for ~15.
It’s for me temporary case while I wait on peak design to be delivered as I mount my phone on motorbike.

text:
the spigen brand is all over the place with the 17 lineup...they had some really quality cases for 16 and 16e and then dropped the ball hard for 17 series imo. for example, the liquid air for 16e was amazing and even included a magnet to make up for the lack of magsafe, then the 17 series version doesn't have magsafe at all? what? i can't even understand that decision making on their end at all

edit: and i really did like the ringke fusion for 16e but charging cutout is really sharp and was incredibly uncomfortable on my pinky...had to return it. i'm guessing it hasn't changed for the 17 series. that really was the main issue otherwise it was a really nice case

author: @cal7447
text: 5:59 Hey Rushfaster, a genuine piece of feedback if any staff happens to read this. I guess you trust that most viewers will notice the channel name and make the natural connections and consequent judgements with no problem, and there's probably no malintent on your part, but I still think it's just bad practice to feature your own product in a round up video with zero acknowledgement for potential conflict of interest. This seems like a common conundrum facing brands that start producing their own products, but NomadNation or PackHacker, for example, will throw in at least one short line of acknowledgement that they are indeed featuring their own product, which is all it takes to make it feel right, imho. I can't be sure on top of my head, but this omission feels like a common occurrence in your videos where your own products are featured. I've been purchasing from your site for years and have always been happy with the quality of the services, so this is just a small complaint, but it has honestly bugged me for quite some time so I thought might as well bring it up.

author: @SierraKilo76
text:
Of course the laptop sleeve will survive a drop test. But what about the device inside?

As far as I am concerned, there are exactly zero laptop sleeves on the market that are practical, easy to use and protective enough for a laptop for US-$ 1000 or more. The last one was the iSaver, which went out of production. A good laptop sleeve that is protective needs to be sturdy enough to distribute an impact on a point and offering some padding. While all offer padding, without a sturdy exterior this is worthless.

text: Hi E, I'm so glad you're finding them useful! Let me know if there are reviews on other items you would find useful as well. I am using the regular pick in large in the video. For a longchamp tote in large, I would chose the large size of this particular bag organizer if you want more space in fitting items around the bag organizer. if you want it completely filled and fitted and more structured looking, you may want to size up to extra large. You should also consider what other tote bags you would use this for so you buy the size that is more versatile.

author: @gen_o
text: I’ve tried several organisers/ inserts but have been only satisfied with  Samorga so far. I have gotten several for probably 90% of my bags, big and small. I opt for the 1.2 mm as my objective is to keep the interiors clean rather than for structure, and it makes so much difference as they’re thin I hardly feel they are there. I like Samorga because the measurements have been spot on in my purchases so far, and you can pick from a whole myriad of colours. I do my own due diligence to double check / triple check measurements + certain allowances I might need vs. my requirements. I also match the colours of my inserts to the interior of my bag — personal preference— and the Samorga offers so much options that I actually enjoy picking out the colours. No issues so far and customer service had been excellent and prompt when I needed to switch out an item for another. The only con I can think of is the hefty shipping costs, so I order mine in bulk/batches, which allow me to do a lot of research first before ordering. For those in the UK, handbag angels in Etsy is also good — they are expanding their selections and they also do bespoke/custom orders (you need to send your bag in for measurements).

text: What style is your favorite? I’m looking for a bag organizer for my Coach Dempsey tote 40 and my Marc Jacobs tote. Is the felt durable?

author: @StephaniesHomestyle
text: I don't think I could choose 🤭🤣 they are all great and durable. The only issue with the felt is it picks up lint. A roller or a fabric shaver is a good investment.👌🏾 I would go with the felt and look up your model to fit for sure it is a bit more structured than the vinyl!
